turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning in Case 14簫8349, Foster v. Chatman. Mr. Bright.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The prosecutors in this case came to court on the morning of jury selection determined to strike all the black prospective jurors.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Mr. Bright, maybe you could address first the 簫簫 the question we raised on Friday with respect to which court certiorari should be directed to.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. We filed this petition originally certiorari to the Supreme Court of Georgia. And of course this Court in Sears v. Upton had issued certiorari 簫簫 this is in 2010 簫簫 to the Supreme Court of Georgia in a similar situation. It appears to us, from looking at this over the weekend, that R.J. Reynolds Tobacco Company v. Durham County, which the Court has decided in eight 簫簫 in 1986, the Court said that, unless there was positive assurance that the decision was not a ruling on the merits, then the writ went to the State supreme court. And the Georgia court, while it has rules and statutes and its own opinions that are not totally in harmony with one another, the rule, nonetheless, is that a certificate of probable cause, which is what was denied in this case, is to be granted if there is arguable merit to the case.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Do you think that affects the scope of our review? In other words, are we addressing just whether there's arguable merit to the claim or are we addressing the claim on its own merits?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Well, I 簫簫 I think what this Court has done in 簫簫 in all these cases is apply Yist v. Nunnemaker to look through to the last reasoned decision, and that would be the decision of the habeas corpus court. In Georgia, typically the habeas court rules, an application is made for certificate of probable cause to the Georgia Supreme Court, and that is often denied summarily. It is denied summarily as it was in this case. It was denied.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I really don't understand that. You 簫簫 you say we would be reversing the Georgia Supreme Court, not the 簫簫 not the habeas court, right? And 簫簫 and all that the Georgia Supreme Court held is that there 簫簫 there 簫簫 is that there was no arguable basis for 簫簫 for its accepting review. So if we reverse that decision, we 簫簫 we tell the Georgia Supreme Court, you're wrong; there is an arguable basis for your accepting review. So we ought to remand to that court, requiring them to accept review, it would seem to me. How can we reverse them on 簫簫 on an issue they 簫簫 they never considered?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Well, that's what happened in R.J. Reynolds. I mean, there you had almost an identical situation where you had an intermediate appellate court that had ruled, and then you had the North Carolina Supreme Court denied review. And the question was, do you issue the writ to the intermediate appellate court or to the North Carolina Supreme Court? And 簫簫 and this Court decided, and Justice Blackman, writing for the court, said, "We want to give practitioners" 簫簫 "We want to end the confusion about this." And so it goes to the State supreme court. There is no difference in our situation here and the situation that R.J. Reynolds 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But 簫簫 but you're saying in that case or in other cases? And if so, which other cases that, in that situation, we, nonetheless, addressed the reasoning of the intermediate court? Is that what you're saying?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: You 簫簫 you did in Sears v. Upton, a case out of Georgia, 561 U.S. 945 in 2010. That was certiorari to the Supreme Court of Georgia, but it came up on exactly the same posture of our 簫簫 our case.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Is there an argument that the petition for certiorari could go to the trial court? I mean, our statute says that it goes to the highest court in which review could have 簫簫 could have been had, I think is the 簫簫 the statutory phrase, in which sounds like the Georgia Supreme Court. On the other hand, as Justice Scalia said, they haven't really directed their attention to the issues before us. I 簫簫 I 簫簫 I'm not sure to me that it's an option to 簫簫 to go to the superior 簫簫 to the Georgia trial court.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Well, let 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Or 簫簫 or is that incorrect?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Well, what this Court has said, both in the R.J. Reynolds case and then that was followed in Grady v. North Carolina last year 簫簫 2015 case, this year, in which, once again, there was an intermediate court decision denied by the 簫簫 the North Carolina Supreme Court. I mean, I can remember all the way back to 1960. There was Thompson v. Louisville, where certiorari was to the police court in Louisville, Kentucky, because no court in Kentucky could take the case because the fine was less than $20. But I think these cases, much more recent, decided by the Court 1986 and this year 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: You're putting together two rules that you say we've established. One is Justice Blackman said, to end the confusion, the petition should be addressed to the Supreme Court. And then you said, we have cases. Look through cases. If 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: 簫簫 the Supreme Court has said just "denied," nothing more than "denied," we look back to the last reasoned decision. Those are both decisions of this Court, and that's what you're relying on.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Well, and 簫簫 and they're not mutually exclusive. This Court can look back through to the last reasoned decision in making its decision in this case, and I believe that's what it should do. But at the same time, the Court's opinions appear to us, on the quick research we did over the weekend on this, that R.J. Reynolds and 簫簫 and the subsequent case say that certiorari would issue to the Georgia Supreme Court. And 簫簫 and we listed it that way, and then when the case was docketed here, it was listed that the lower court was the superior court of Butts County.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: What if the State supreme court wrote a short opinion and said, We're not going to determine whether there was, in fact 簫簫 the only issue we're going to determine is whether there's any arguable merit to this? And then you say that the whole issue of whether it was a correct application in Batson is 簫簫 is the issue that we have to decide?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: I 簫簫 I 簫簫 I think in R.J. Reynolds, I think that's this Court's law, yes.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Could I ask you another 簫簫 a question about another preliminary issue before you get to the 簫簫 the 簫簫 the underlying question in the case? The Superior Court said, on page 175 of the Joint Appendix, that the issue of the Batson violation was not reviewable based on the doctrine of res judicata. And then it later said, and this is 192 of the Joint Appendix, that it will review the Batson claim as to whether Petitioner has shown any change in the facts sufficient to overcome the res judicata bar. Now, if you put those two together, you could argue that the superior court decided only a question of State law, namely, whether the situation here was such that there could be review of the Batson claim. What is your response to that?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Well, the State doesn't argue that. And I think the reason for that is because the Court said, we're moving 簫簫 the Court is going to address step three of Batson. And it said, Foster's Batson claim is without merit.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, but is it question of Federal or State law as to whether or not the Petitioner has shown a change in facts sufficient to overcome the res judicata bar? The one 簫簫 the page 192 language that Justice Alito quoted, is that 簫簫 is that a State law question or 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: That's a State law question. And here 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: 簫簫 the Court decided it, but I 簫簫 I point out, Justice 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, if it's a State law question and they resolved it against you, what do you have to 簫簫 then what do you have to argue?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: No, no, no.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I think you have to say it's a Federal question.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: No. In order to decide it, the 簫簫 this is exactly like Ake v. Oklahoma, where the court, the Oklahoma court had to decide the Federal question in order to decide whether it had jurisdiction over the issue. And this Court held in Ake that, where the court has to decide the Federal issue 簫簫 and it did in this case. It clearly decided the Federal issue and felt that the 簫簫 found that the Batson claim had no merit. So it is decided, the Federal issue, and there's no contest about that in the 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Explain to me why deciding the Federal issue was essential to its deciding the State res judicata issue.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Because it 簫簫 it 簫簫 it framed the question as being that it would look at the Ake v. 簫簫 excuse me 簫簫 they would look at the Batson v. Kentucky claim and that, if there was merit to that claim, then the court would grant the writ on it. On the other hand, if it found that there was not merit on it, then 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: There 簫簫 you think it was saying whether there's res judicata or not depends on whether the new claim has any merit?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: I think 簫簫 I think it's exactly 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's a very strange application of res judicata, it seems to me.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I thought it was whether there 簫簫 there were changed facts sufficient enough.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Well, the Georgia law is that you can bring an issue that's been litigated already before direct appeal.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Right. Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT

We didn't 簫簫 it wasn't 簫簫 since the State had raised this, either in their opposition to cert or in their brief, it's not briefed before this Court, but I think that's the deciding case on this.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thanks, counsel. I think we have your argument on the point. Thank you.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Okay. Thank you very much. If I could just say what happened here was that the prosecutors had identified the African簫Americans by race, they had rated them against each other in case it came down to having to select a black juror.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The prosecutors said the reason for concentrating on the black jurors was that you had informed them you would present a Batson challenge, and therefore, it was necessary for them to see if there was a race簫neutral ground for disqualifying that case.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Right. Two answers to that, Justice Ginsburg. I mean, what the lawyers did here was these lawyers have practiced here for a long time in Rome, Georgia. They said the prosecutor always strikes all the blacks from the jury. That's been the historic practice. We think they're going to strike all the blacks on the jury in our case. But last year the Supreme Court of the United States decided Batson v. Kentucky, and we asked the Court not to let that happen in this case. Now, of course, if the prosecutor is willing to avoid a Batson challenge, they could have not discriminated. That would have been the first thing to do. But 簫簫 but secondly, with regard to the information that's collected here, it doesn't seem like it's information just to exercise strikes when they say, if it comes down to having to take an African簫American, Ms. Hardge, or in another place, Ms. Garrett, might be okay. And the district attorney himself said Marilyn Garrett has the most potential of the black prospective jurors. In other words, the blacks were taken out of the picture here. They were taken and 簫簫 and dealt with separately. And over the weekend, the jury 簫簫 the questioning ended on a Friday. And the judge said, all right. Over the weekend, you've got your chance to decide who you're going to strike. And they knew exactly who they were going to strike because the 簫簫 the 簫簫 the jurors are listed in order. The State goes first, and if it accepts a juror, then the State accepts, and that juror is on. There's no going back. There's no backstriking or there's no striking people here and there. They developed three strike lists. And one of those strike lists was a list headed "Definite NOs." These are the people absolutely are not going to be on this jury. There are only six jurors listed on the list of "Definite NOs," and the first five are African簫Americans. The sixth is a juror who made clear during the voir dire process that she could not impose the death penalty under any circumstances. The State moved to strike her for cause. The judge probably erred in not granting that strike. But even she ranked behind the black jurors in terms of the priorities that the prosecution had for 簫簫 for striking.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Counsel, at this 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. Bright 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: 簫簫 at the time, Mr. Lanier said they weren't striking the 簫簫 the jurors because of race. They were striking them because they were women. And I guess three 簫簫 three out of the four 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: 簫簫 African簫Americans were 簫簫 who were struck were 簫簫 were women. How does that 簫簫 and then that explanation is just kind of fallen out of the case. How 簫簫 how does that affect the analysis?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Well, he 簫簫 he did accept women, though, as 簫簫 as well. If 簫簫 bear with me a 簫簫 just a moment.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The Court had not yet held that Batson applies to 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: The Court had not held in JEB that Batson applied to women, but the Court did say in JEB that, of course, it could be used as a pretext, women, for striking on the basis of race. In this case, the prosecutor struck three white jurors, and then he struck the three black jurors, women, the three black women and the three white women. The final 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. 簫簫 Mr. Bright, Mr. Lanier 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 answered yes when he asked 簫簫 when during the 簫簫 during the trial when he was asked whether he had done 簫簫 I don't know if it 簫簫 oh, no, it was on the motion for a new trial hearing 簫簫 whether he had done the same extensive background check on all the jurors, white and black. Did you find any evidence of that extensive black background search?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: No. The only 簫簫 what that's talking about 簫簫 and 簫簫 and the investigator said this in his deposition, was the color 簫簫 race簫coded colored list, those first four lists you have in the Joint Appendix in which the blacks are marked with a "B" and are highlighted in green with a marker up at the corner saying green designates black.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So 簫簫 so your 簫簫 your understanding of that statement was that all 簫簫 he had only done an extensive search on the blacks on the list?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Well, it's clear, Mr. Lundy had prepared a 簫簫 a list, notes in which he talked about just the black jurors in 簫簫 in the case. And I think the State concedes in its brief that the focus was on the black jurors.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: During the trial, did defense counsel, when he made his initial Batson challenge 簫簫 not in the papers, but at trial 簫簫 did he again say that this was part and parcel of the prosecutor's pattern?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: He didn't say that, but I point this interesting thing out. When they discussed the Batson motion before trial, there was never a suggestion that there wouldn't be a Batson hearing. Everybody knew what was going to happen, that all the blacks would be struck, and then they'd have a hearing after that happened. But the defense had basically put their motion in writing and relied upon that throughout the jury selection.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I was just surprised that we didn't hear about this preparation for a Batson hearing until 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 the habeas.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: The 簫簫 the defense lawyers at trial did move for the prosecution's notes. And the prosecution opposed that. They're very strict in not 簫簫 not giving up their notes. Then when the prosecutor testified on the motion for a new trial, he did something I've never seen a lawyer do before. He cut a bargain, sort of, with the judge and the lawyer saying, I will testify, but only if I don't have to show them my notes. I mean, basic rules of evidence are you testify and rely upon notes; the other side can see the notes. But here, these notes were guarded until 2006, when we obtained them through a Freedom of Information or what they call Open Records Act in Georgia.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The prosecutors said that you 簫簫 they said, we never 簫簫 we never wrote or authorized or relied on those notes. And you didn't call the prosecutors to test the veracity of that assertion.

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: No, but all the prosecutor talked about were the color簫highlighted notes. Each prosecutor filed an affidavit which are in the 簫簫 in the Joint Appendix at 168. And all they said was, we didn't highlight it in green, and we didn't tell anybody else to highlight it in green. And then Mr. Lanier says 簫簫 and I don't have anything else to say beyond what I said at the Batson hearing and the motion for a new trial. Mr. Pullen said 簫簫 the only other thing he said is, I didn't use those green簫highlighted lists in choosing the jury; but, of course, that's just the first few pages. What's damning about this is not so much that, but the "Definite NOs" list, the misrepresentation to the trial court that Ms. Garrett 簫簫 that they wanted Ms. Garrett. That's what they told the trial court. And the trial court relied upon that in denying the Batson motion, that this showed their openness to having 簫簫 Ms. Garrett was on the "Definite NO" list. She was on each of the strike lists. Ms. Garrett was never in the running to be on this jury. But they represented to the court that because another African簫American, Shirley Powell, was excused for cause 簫簫 there were five African簫Americans in the venire at the start when they got ready to instruct the jury. But one said, turns out I know somebody in the family. She was excused for cause. And the prosecutors said 簫簫 made it 簫簫 implied clearly that had it not been for that, that extra strike, that Ms. Garrett would have sat. At the same time 簫簫 and they're still arguing this both ways, that they both wanted her and didn't want her 簫簫 they give eleven reasons for why Ms. Garrett would not be a good juror. That she's impudent and she doesn't respect the court. If you believe all the things they said about her, they would never want her as a juror. But those things, I would submit, are not really valid in terms of 簫簫 in terms of the reasons, because the reasons they gave here, many were demonstrably false and not supported by the evidence, including reasons they gave about Ms. Garrett. They were inconsistent, some were completely incredible, and they applied to white jurors 簫簫 some of these reasons applied to white jurors who had the same characteristics as the African簫Americans who were struck. And then lastly, and what's so important under Miller簫El, they didn't question the jurors about the reasons for striking them. They gave reasons for striking, and one question would have cleared up some of these. And Miller簫El says that the failure to engage in any meaningful voir dire about whatever your reason is, is evidence suggesting that the explanation is a sham and a pretext.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. Bright 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: And 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 I have found some circuit courts who have a rule on appeal or on habeas which is if they can find one legitimate reason for striking a juror 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 that's enough to defeat a Batson challenge. Do you believe that's an appropriate rule? Are you suggesting a different approach to the question?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Well, it can't 簫簫 I 簫簫 I would suggest it 簫簫 it can't possibly be. Because this Court said in Justice Alito's opinion in Snyder v. Louisiana that where the peremptory strike was shown to have been motivated in substantial part by race, that it could not be sustained. And 簫簫 excuse me 簫簫 I 簫簫 I would suggest to you, it shouldn't even really say substantial. Because if this Court, as it said so many times, is engaged in unceasing efforts to end race discrimination in the criminal courts, then a strike that 簫簫 strikes motivated by race cannot be tolerable. And, of course, as 簫簫 as pointed out here in the 簫簫 in the amici, this is a serious problem, not just in this case, but in other cases where people come to court with their canned reasons and just read them off. That happened in this case, where one of the reasons that was given was just taken verbatim out of a 簫簫 two of the reasons given were taken verbatim out of a reported case. So you don't have the reason for the lawyer in this case. He said my personal preference. It wasn't his personal preference. It was the personal preference of some U.S. attorney in Mississippi who gave that reason, and then it was upheld on appeal by 簫簫 by the Fifth Circuit. But I 簫簫 we would suggest that the standard is at least what Snyder says, because when you have both 簫簫 you can always have, as Miller簫El recognized 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, in 簫簫 in response to Justice Sotomayor's question 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: 簫簫 if the prosecutor argues a laundry list of reasons for striking the black juror and some of those are reasonable and some are implausible, how should the Court approach the Batson analysis?

turns.name: STEPHEN B. BRIGHT
turns.text: I think the Court looks at which reasons are pretextual. I think the fact that there is a laundry list suggests in and of itself that the Court should scrutinize the reasons very carefully, should be suspect of the reasons. Because otherwise, what the Court is going to do is just simply encourage prosecutors or any party in a case, since Batson applies to everyone 簫簫 is going to encourage a party to just give as many reasons as possible and hope that one will be acceptable.
